title: Arrays & Loops
description: Slices are Go's workhorse collection. Master slice creation, append, range loops, and 2D slices — these appear in nearly every interview problem.

sections.title: Slices and append
sections.explanation: Go arrays have fixed length, but **slices** are dynamic. Create with a literal `[]int{...}` or `make([]int, len, cap)`. **append()** adds elements and returns a new slice (always reassign the result). Slices are reference types — they point to an underlying array.

exercise.prompt: Create a slice `nums` with values [10, 20, 30]. Append 40 and 50. Create a zero slice of length 3 using make. Print all three: nums, its length, and the zero slice.

validation.message: Slice should have 5 elements after append, zeros should be [0 0 0]
Use append() to add elements and make() for zero-initialized slices

exercise.hint: nums = append(nums, 40, 50) adds two elements. make([]int, 3) creates [0 0 0].

sections.title: Range loops
sections.explanation: **for i, v := range slice** gives both index and value. Use **_** to discard the index or value. Go has only one loop keyword — **for** — which covers while-loops, C-style for-loops, and range iteration. Interviewers expect you to pick the right form for each situation.

exercise.prompt: Given `fruits := []string{"apple", "banana", "cherry"}`, use a range loop to print each fruit with its index in the format `0: apple`. Then use a second range loop with _ to print just the values.

validation.expected:
0: apple
1: banana
2: cherry
apple
banana
cherry
validation.message: First loop prints index: value, second prints just values
Use range for iteration and Printf for formatted output

exercise.hint: fmt.Printf("%d: %s\n", i, v) formats the index and value. Use _ to discard the index in the second loop.

sections.title: Slice operations
sections.explanation: Slicing with **s[low:high]** creates a sub-slice (half-open interval, excluding high). **copy()** makes a true copy to avoid shared memory. **append(s[:i], s[i+1:]...)** removes element at index i. These are essential for in-place array manipulation problems.

exercise.prompt: Given `nums := []int{10, 20, 30, 40, 50}`, extract a sub-slice from index 1 to 3 (inclusive of 1, exclusive of 4). Then remove the element at index 2 (value 30) from the original. Print the sub-slice and the modified nums.

validation.message: Sub-slice should be [20 30 40], modified slice should skip 30
Use slice notation and append with spread for removal

exercise.hint: sub := nums[1:4] gets elements at indices 1,2,3. nums = append(nums[:2], nums[3:]...) removes index 2.

sections.title: 2D slices
sections.explanation: Go has no built-in 2D arrays for dynamic sizes. Build them as a **slice of slices**: allocate the outer slice, then each row individually. This pattern appears in grid/matrix problems like BFS on a board, DP tables, and image rotation.

exercise.prompt: Create a 2x3 grid (2 rows, 3 columns) initialized to zeros. Set grid[0][2] = 7 and grid[1][0] = 4. Print the grid.

validation.message: Grid should be 2x3 with 7 at [0][2] and 4 at [1][0]
Use make for both the outer slice and each inner row

exercise.hint: make([][]int, rows) creates the outer slice. Each grid[i] = make([]int, cols) creates a row. Set grid[0][2] = 7 and grid[1][0] = 4.
